data.title: Upstart: Volatile Revenue And High Valuations Should Make It Not Worth Your Interest
data.description:
As Upstart (NASDAQ:UPST) stock continues on its upward trajectory post-earnings release, I wanted to take a look at the company. I’ve had some interest in the platform / Fintech industry so I was wondering if this stock would fit in my portfolio. As a cloud-based artificial intelligence lending platform, Upstart could ride many of the larger macro secular trends. This article will focus on some key financial metrics in order to build a viewpoint on Upstart.

Quarter and Full-year financial analysis

At first glance, looking at the company’s most recently released financial results shows that Upstart is growing its business pretty well. In Q4 2024, total revenue was $219 which was a 56% increase compared to the same time last year and a 35% increase compared to last quarter. While this headline figure looks nice, it does include the volatile section of “Interest income, interest expense, and fair value adjustments”. This was up by $19.6 million this quarter yet down by a loss of $12.5 million a year ago creating the large revenue growth. Looking strictly at the core business, i.e. Platform fee revenue, this was $199 million, posting a lower but still great 30% year-over-year and 19% last quarter growth.

It is important for investors to focus on Fee revenue, as this is Upstart’s core business. According to the company’s 10-K, “Interest income, interest expense, and fair value adjustments, net can fluctuate based on the fair value of financial instruments held on our consolidated balance sheets. This amount has historically been a small percentage of our total revenue, and we do not manage our business with a focus on growing this component of revenue”. In other words, while Upstart does hold a certain percentage of loans in its portfolio, roughly 16% is based on the most recent fillings. It earns income and losses by holding these loans, which can be rather volatile and skews revenue growth figures.

Running the same analysis on the company’s full-year 2024 results, Upstart revenue increased by 24% year-over-year to $637 million. However, its total fee revenue only increased by 13% year-over-year to $635 million. This is somewhat concerning as fee revenue growth has been volatile. Historical results show 18% growth, -9% decline, 14% growth, and 30% growth year-over-year in Q1 2024, Q2 202,4 and Q3 2024 respectively. I am hoping that a high growth rate (maybe not as high as 30%) is sustainable moving forward. Even looking at historical revenue, it can be seen that annual growth has been uneven, with the company hitting a revenue high-mark of $907 million in 2022. Looking for consistent growth is something to watch out for in the coming quarters, as it is hard to justify premium valuations on uneven/high-risk revenue.

In its earnings call, management attributed the current quarter's high growth to “model enhancements, a lower Upstart Macro Index, and rate cuts”. The latter two have to do with US economic conditions and interest rates. From my understanding, these are baked into Upstart’s AI and risk model calculations, which affect the loans offered on the company’s platform. But the dependency of results based on US macroeconomic conditions and interest rates, as well as the volatility of revenue results are Key risks to keep in mind.

Rounding out this analysis is that Upstart, reported a GAAP net loss of -$129 million, which was a large improvement from the -$240 million reported in 2023. Adjusted EBITDA was $10.6 million, up from the loss of $17.2 million in 2023. As noted in the press release by CEO Dave Girouard, the business achieved Adjusted EBITDA levels not seen since Q1 2022 and is close to being back to breakeven from a GAAP standpoint. In my view, this is great news for Upstart. as it shows management is on track toward profitably growing and scaling its AI-based platform. But once again this needs to be questioned should revenue experience some downside volatility.

Upstart Avenues of Growth and Risks

So the key question I have is whether or not Upstart can hit and sustain high levels of growth in order to justify its status as a “growth Fintech” company. One way to see how the company is doing is by looking at certain reported KPIs or “key performance indicators”. These are, in my view, a means of determining whether or not the company is on solid operational footing to hit its long-term goals.

For the full year 2024, Upstart gave out 697,092 loans, which was an improvement of 59.3% compared to the 437,659 number of loans the same time last year. However, when translating to actual dollar amounts, the company’s transaction volume for full-year 2024 was $5.930 billion, which was only 27.7% higher than the $4.645 billion reported. This implies that the average size of the loans issued actually decreased year-over-year. In 2024, the average loan size of the company was $8,507.89 compared to $10,608.93 in 2023, a decrease of 19.8%.

In the last quarter of 2024, the company had 245,663 loans worth $2.107 billion with an average size of $8,579.86. Therefore, looking at Q4 data, I believe that the increase in loan volume for the year was due to getting smaller valued loans. Typically, I would be worried about profitability in the case where the dollar value of the loans is decreasing. However, since the Upstart loan process is fully automated for 91% of its loans, I am not too worried about it. I believe this is part of Upstart’s ability to scale its revenue on its platform as it can target customers for lower loan values with itself having almost zero processing costs due to automation.

Looking over to the future, Upstart has also launched a new initiative to target an entirely new market segment. The company recently launched late last year its T-Prime program which targets customers with credit scores above 720. These customers have much better credit scores than Upstart’s usual customer base and are already serviced by banks and other fintech companies, making it a highly competitive market. The offset to the competitiveness of this market is the fact that these “super prime” customers comprise almost half of all Americans, making it a very large market. It makes sense to expand to this market due to the bulk of Upstart’s loans being automated, therefore the company can absorb a potentially lower margin.

According to Upstart’s press release, the company can differentiate itself via its AI platform, leading to more competitive rates and automated approvals. This program will allow Upstart’s lender partners to tap into this larger, lucrative market. Since the approval process of 90% of Upstart loans is automated, this should lead to significant operating efficiencies and cost savings for Upstart’s lending partners, which tend to be smaller banks, credit unions, and other institutions. For customers, on the other hand, this could translate to easy credit with zero documents to upload and zero wait times.

To be honest, I am in a “wait and see” mode when it comes to this initiative due to the extremely high levels of competition. Targeting the “super prime” customer is a bit outside of Upstart’s competitive advantage of using AI to better analyze credit worthiness of people with sub-par credit scores. I believe the company could make some incremental revenue from this segment, but I am uncertain if it will be a significant amount. However, if Upstart could make inroads with this segment, it could be a total game-changer for the company.

Upstart Key Concentration Risk

Apart from the volatility of its Revenue sources discussed above, another major risk factor for Upstart is its concentration risk on the Lender side of its platform. Based on the latest 10-Q disclosure, Upstart’s top three lending partners collectively originated 86% of the Transaction Volume or 71% of total revenue. This is an increase from the 80% Transaction Volume or 63% of total revenue from the full year 2023. Concentration risk refers to a situation where a company is reliant on a handful of suppliers or partners.

This situation is rather unusual, in my view, as there are more than 100 Lenders on the platform, so I initially thought that loan distribution would not be so concentrated. Even more concerning, according to the disclosures, there are no minimum commitments for these lending partners. Therefore, Upstart’s revenue could be impacted should they limit or cease operations. This is especially true if these lending partners decide to invest in their own technology.

In order to mitigate this risk, Upstart is continuing to sign up new lending partners in order to grow its number of lending partners and diversify its loan origination source. In January to today alone, the company signed up Pelican State Credit Union and Sandia Area Federal Credit Union. According to the press release, Pelican State is the largest state-chartered credit union in Louisiana with over 50,000 members while Sandia Area is a leading New Mexico credit union that has 90,000 members and assets worth $1.2 billion. Signing these lending partners is good news for Upstart, however it doesn’t guarantee that they will be a major source of loans. Therefore this concentration risk is something that investors should keep an eye out for.

Conclusion and Valuation

In terms of valuations, Upstart in my view is on the expensive side. The company is trading at a Forward P/E ratio of 65x, which is much higher than the Financial sector median of 11.55x. Given that Upstart is only forecasted to reach GAAP profitability next year, looking at a multiple that focuses on revenue generation could be appropriate when looking at a fast-growing company like Upstart. The two multiples typically used for this are the P/S and EV/R ratios, which are 8.3x and 9x respectively. These ratios are much higher than the Financial sector median of 2.8x and 3.3x, respectively.

These ratios were post-earnings release and reflect a company that is trading at a premium and is rather expensive in my view. Typically, this premium could be justified for tech firms that are scaling. However, as discussed, the revenue growth of Upstart is volatile and in fact the company has not returned to its revenue high of $907 million in 2022.

An argument can be made that Upstart deserves this premium to industry, as the Financial sector industry includes traditional banks. However even when comparing Upstart to its direct peers, the company has one of the highest TTM sales multiples as seen in the graph below. When looking at FinTech platforms that are consumer-focused, I believe that the “leader” of the industry is SoFi (SOFI) which has nearly double the market cap of Upstart. SoFi is growing at a faster rate than Upstart and is trading at a 5x forward P/S ratio, which is lower than 8.3x of the latter.

Given the factors discussed above when comparing to the wider industry and its more direct peers, in my view, Upstart is rather expensive and not worth its premium price. I have Upstart at a neutral rating, as I will wait and see.

data.title: Meta Platforms: Waiting For A Significant Milestone, Avoid Making The Mistake Of Selling Out
data.description:
It's been nearly four months since my last analysis of Meta Platforms (NASDAQ:META). Despite a substantial 22% increase in the stock price, I’m maintaining my "hold" rating. While the company has strong growth potential in long-term, the stock's all-time high remains a key limiting factor.

Thesis Explanation

My previous article was titled No Longer Just Facebook, But Still Priced Like Facebook, which perfectly captures my core investment thesis for holding Meta shares. Not much has changed over the past quarter, especially since my investment perspective is strictly long-term. I still see Meta as a unique opportunity to own two businesses for the price of one.

When investing, I aim to strike a balance between long-term holding and valuation-based position management. I also favor a mix of fundamental and quantitative analysis, with a stronger emphasis on the latter. However, Meta tempts me to break some of my usual principles and consider a "hold forever" rating for three key reasons: its exceptional business efficiency based on financial performance metrics, its strong solvency, and the potential undervaluation when evaluating the Family of Apps and Reality Labs segments separately.

Q4 and 2024 Report Summary

To verify this characteristic and double-check the latest data, let's turn to the recently released report for the 4th quarter and the whole of 2024.

In Q4 2024, Meta once again grew its daily active users, reaching 3.35 billion people using at least one of its apps.

On one hand, that’s already 41% of the world’s population—an impressive figure, especially considering that not everyone is part of social media’s target audience. At first glance, this growth might seem to be approaching its natural limit.

On the other hand, the global population is also expanding, albeit at a slower rate. Over the past two years, Meta’s active user base has grown by 390 million (+13%), while the world’s population has increased by nearly 2%, adding approximately 141 million people.

The 14% year-over-year increase in the average cost per ad (p 13 of presentation) drove Average Revenue per Person (ARPP) to a record $14.25 (p. 11), marking a nearly 15.5% YoY jump. This, in turn, fueled yet another record-breaking net profit—something we, as Meta shareholders, have grown accustomed to in recent years. The company surpassed the $20 billion mark in a single quarter, with YoY growth hitting an impressive 48.6%.

Meta continues to outperform expectations, solidifying its dominance in the social media advertising market with a staggering 38% net profit margin and exceptional returns on both total and invested capital—37% and 36%, respectively.

On top of that, the company remains solvent. While net debt is positive at the end of Q4 (Meta holds nearly $44 billion in cash against $28.8 billion in long-term debt and $21 billion in capital leases) the company spends just $715 million on interest payments annually —only 1% of operating income!

Where Does Meta Spend This Money?

Capital expenditures almost reached $40 billion in 2024, but this is far from the limit. Continuing to invest in both AI and in supporting the core business, the company plans to spend $60-65 billion on investments in 2025.

Meta Platforms remains committed to heavy investment in Reality Labs, with total spending on VR and AR expected to exceed $100 billion by 2025. The Reality Labs division is responsible for developing cutting-edge VR and AR products, including the Quest headset lineup and Ray-Ban Stories smart glasses. CEO Mark Zuckerberg has called 2025 a pivotal year for smart glasses, emphasizing the development of AI-powered eyewear in partnership with EssilorLuxottica.

In Q4 2024, Reality Labs posted a record quarterly revenue of $1.08 billion. For the full year, revenue reached $2.15 billion, reflecting a 13% year-over-year increase. However, the division's operating loss climbed to $4.97 billion in 2024, driven by substantial investments in R&D for next-generation augmented reality products.

Despite ongoing losses, Reality Labs exceeded nearly all of its 2024 sales and user growth targets. While Meta hasn’t shared detailed figures, two key statements from management are particularly noteworthy.

Meta CFO Susan Li said in a recent earnings call that Meta planned to continue to invest significantly in Reality Labs because it`s a "strategic long-term priority." The company's chief technology officer, Andrew Bosworth, states that "2024 was a pivotal year for Reality Labs and positioned us for greatness in 2025"

Now, we get to the most interesting part.

I’m not a technical expert or an insider, so I don’t have any more insight into Reality Labs’ plans and numbers than the market does. However, I’ve picked up on the not-so-subtle hints from management—this division is central to Meta’s future. It’s no coincidence that the company breaks its business into two distinct segments in its reports, showcasing the financials of both the Family of Apps and Reality Labs separately.

To me, this reinforces the idea that by holding Meta shares, I’m essentially a co-owner of two businesses.

My 2 Cents About Meta Valuation

Meta shares are trading at 30x earnings. The EPS diluted CAGR over the past 3 years was 20%, and the expected growth rate for the next 3 years is slightly more modest - 12% - but still double-digit.

While Meta's current valuation is above the sector median, it still seems more than reasonable. Compared to its historical metrics, the stock is also trading at a premium—the three-year median P/E is around 25.4, implying a ~15% overvaluation. Normally, I’d take this into account, but with Meta, I see things differently.

Let’s revisit my hypothesis that Apps and Reality Labs are essentially two businesses bundled together—and run some quick calculations. I realize this is a simplification, and I welcome your thoughts in the comments, but hear me out.

At a 30x valuation, Meta’s $1.84 trillion market cap is based on $62 billion in net profit (rounded for simplicity). Reality Labs, despite being unprofitable, is still part of the equation. Companies like TSLA and TWLO, while operating at a loss, have been valued at 20–30x annual revenue at their peaks. Taking the conservative end of that range and factoring in Reality Labs' ~$2 billion in annual revenue, we get a rough (pretty rough I guess) estimate of $40 billion for its value.

Now, here’s why this matters: if we subtract this $40 billion from Meta’s total market cap, along with the segment’s projected ~$18 billion loss, we’re left with a core business valued at just 22.5x earnings:

(1.840B−40B)/(62B+18B)=22.5x

That’s not just reasonable—it’s outright cheap.

Risks To My Thesis

I’m sure my perspective will have its critics, but I wanted to share the thought process that’s been running through my mind. Right now, there are no glaring downsides to Meta’s business for me. The company boasts the highest margins, strong solvency, and impressive efficiency.

The biggest risk is the rising capital expenditures, which I wrote about above, especially if developments in AI, the metaverse, and Reality Labs don’t unfold as positively as hoped, which could slow profit growth. We’ve seen forecasts predicting a decline in growth by 2028, and even a potential negative outlook for 2031 (though it’s still early, and the forecast isn’t widely covered). In that case, Reality Labs may not become the exceptional growth driver we’re hoping for, and could even drag the entire business down. However, given the level of technology and investment already in play, it’s hard to imagine that scenario unfolding.

Bottom Line

I’ve shared my perspective on Meta’s current situation. Right now, I see the company as having two parts: the highly profitable and efficient Apps business, which generates 98.5% of Meta’s revenue, and the high-risk Reality Labs segment which is still generating significant losses. According to management, Reality Labs is expected to drive the next phase of growth in the coming years. From this viewpoint, I believe holding Meta shares at current prices makes sense, as you’re essentially getting the company's second business, Reality Labs, as a bonus.

data.title: Janus Henderson Short Duration Flexible Bond Fund Q4 2024 Commentary
data.description:
Investment environment

The U.S. short-term fixed income market posted a slightly negative return for the quarter, with the Bloomberg 1-3 Year US Government/Credit Index returning -0.02%. Credit spread sectors outperformed U.S. Treasuries.

The U.S. economy remained resilient, as gross domestic product (GDP) growth in the third quarter of 2024 came in at 2.8% and the unemployment rate held steady around 4.1%. While Treasury markets sold off, risk markets reacted favorably to the decisive Republican victory in the November election, due to expectations for expansionary fiscal policies and deregulation. However, after starting the rate-cutting cycle with a 50- basis-point (bps) cut in September, the Federal Reserve (Fed) took a decidedly more hawkish tone at its December meeting, with the revised Fed dot plot implying just 50 bps of rate cuts in 2025. The yield on the two-year U.S. Treasury (US2Y) ended the quarter at 4.24% relative to 3.64% at the end of September. Investors weighed the expected positive economic implications of the incoming administration with the slower anticipated pace of rate cuts. Corporate investment-grade credit spreads tightened 9 bps to 80 bps, while high-yield spreads tightened 8 bps to 287 bps.

Portfolio review

We strive to generate moderate income from credit spread sectors while limiting drawdowns. We seek to do this by constructing the portfolio with credit risk and a modest amount of interest-rate risk over the cycle.

Our overweight allocation to spread risk contributed to relative performance. Spread sectors benefited relative results on the back of relatively healthy economic data and positive investor sentiment following the outcome of the November election.

The key contributors included overweight allocations to securitized credit sectors such as non-agency mortgages, commercial mortgage-backed securities ('CMBS'), and asset- backed securities (ABS). Security selection within investment-grade corporates also contributed, while an allocation to agency MBS detracted.

We trimmed our overall spread risk allocation – primarily via a reduction in investment-grade corporates, which are trading at richer valuations relative to other sectors – as spreads tightened during the quarter. Loan exposure increased during the quarter, while we maintained our significant overweight exposure to securitized credit sectors and trimmed our agency MBS exposure slightly.

Within our interest-rate risk exposure, we entered the quarter with a modest duration overweight, which detracted as Treasury yields rose. We added duration as yields rose and continue to favor an overweight to duration especially at the short end of the yield curve, as we believe the market may be pricing in too few rate cuts in 2025, and in case the economy weakens more than expected.

The portfolio remains well diversified with exposure to an array of fixed income sectors, while still limiting our exposure to below-investment-grade investments. This portfolio structure seeks to capture the benefits of diversification and attractive relative value opportunities while limiting downside risk.

Manager outlook

As we enter 2025, we believe investors should seek to take advantage of three positive drivers impacting fixed income markets: the strong U.S. economy, the Fed having initiated its rate-cutting cycle, and the likely normalization of rate- spread correlations due to lower inflation.

Regarding the U.S. economy, corporates and consumers remain largely resilient. While corporate spreads are tight, we believe that strong fundamentals – including the favorable macroeconomic environment and reasonable leverage levels – will continue to support these valuations. Additionally, securitized sectors have continued to trade cheaply relative to corporates, offering attractive yields and the potential for spread tightening.

Furthermore, now that the Fed has begun its rate-cutting cycle and rates markets have priced in a slower pace of cuts, we believe bond investors may lean into the yields on offer and be less concerned with fighting rising rates. Even if inflation proves to be stickier due to the incoming administration’s economic policies, we think much of this risk is already priced in.

Real-time data indicates that inflation has continued to moderate toward the central bank’s target of 2%, and we believe that a resurgence of inflation above 3% is unlikely. Rates and spreads historically have been negatively correlated when inflation is below 3% – resulting in reduced portfolio volatility, as any material widening in credit spreads may be somewhat counterbalanced by falling rates.

Overall, we favor an overweight to credit spread risk amid the strong fundamental and technical backdrop, and an overweight to duration risk due to expectations for further rate cuts and a re-steepening of the yield curve. The market is currently pricing in a terminal rate around 4%, which is notably higher than the Fed’s estimate of 3.1%. We think the actual terminal rate will settle at a level below 4%, but we also expect the Fed’s estimate to move higher.

We believe it is important for investors to maximize income per unit of risk and limit drawdown risks in the present environment. In our view, the current market is more of a “carry” and bond pickers market, where investors are rewarded by clipping relatively high coupons and picking individual bonds with positively skewed risk-return expectations, as opposed to making macro bets.

Moreover, we think allocating to sectors that are trading at cheaper relative valuations – such as loans over high yield, or collateralized loan obligations and ABS over corporates – will be key in 2025. There likely will be continued noise and volatility around the new administration’s policies on deregulation, taxes, tariffs, and immigration, which we believe may create alpha opportunities for nimble, active investment managers.

Portfolio management

Greg Wilensky, CFA | Michael Keough

Definitions

Basis point (BP) equals 1/100 of a percentage point. 1 bp = 0.01%, 100 bps = 1%.

Duration measures a bond price’s sensitivity to changes in interest rates. The longer a bond’s duration, the higher its sensitivity to changes in interest rates and vice versa. A yield curve plots the yields (interest rate) of bonds with equal credit quality but differing maturity dates. Typically bonds with longer maturities have higher yields.

Credit spread is the difference in yield between securities with similar maturity but different credit quality. Widening spreads generally indicate deteriorating creditworthiness of corporate borrowers, and narrowing indicate improving.

2-year Treasury yield is the interest rate on U.S. Treasury bonds that will mature two years from the date of purchase.

Quantitative tightening (QT) is a government monetary policy occasionally used to decrease the money supply by either selling government securities or letting them mature and removing them from its cash balances.

Volatility measures risk using the dispersion of returns for a given investment.

Diversification neither assures a profit nor eliminates the risk of experiencing investment losses.

Monetary policy refers to the policies of a central bank, aimed at influencing the level of inflation and growth in an economy. It includes controlling interest rates and the supply of money. Correlation measures the degree to which two variables move in relation to each other. A value of 1.0 implies movement in parallel, -1.0 implies movement in opposite directions, and 0.0 implies no relationship.

Please consider the charges, risks, expenses and investment objectives carefully before investing. The opinions are as of 12/31/24, are subject to change and may not reflect the views of others in the organization. Janus Henderson may have a business relationship with certain entities discussed. The comments should not be construed as a recommendation of individual holdings or market sectors, but as an illustration of broader themes.

Mutual fund performance for some periods may incorporate returns from older share classes, adjusted for fees and expenses. Visit janushenderson.com/synthetic for specific details. Holdings are subject to change without notice.

Performance attribution is a method used to analyze and quantify the performance of an investment portfolio relative to its benchmark and does not represent actual performance. Attribution is calculated using gross returns before the deduction of advisory fees and considers differences in weighting for the portfolio and the index. This analysis aims to break down the portfolio's performance into different components to understand what factors contributed to relative return, among other performance characteristics. The analysis can provide insights into which decisions positively or negatively impacted the portfolio's performance and serves as the basis for this discussion.

There is no assurance the stated objective(s) will be met.

Investing involves risk, including the possible loss of principal and fluctuation of value.

Fixed income securities are subject to interest rate, inflation, credit and default risk. The bond market is volatile. As interest rates rise, bond prices usually fall, and vice versa. The return of principal is not guaranteed, and prices may decline if an issuer fails to make timely payments or its credit strength weakens.

High-yield or "junk" bonds involve a greater risk of default and price volatility and can experience sudden and sharp price swings.

Derivatives can be more volatile and sensitive to economic or market changes than other investments, which could result in losses exceeding the original investment and magnified by leverage.

ESG Integration. As part of its investment process, portfolio management considers ESG risks and opportunities (“ESG Factors”) that it believes are financially material, alongside other fundamental investment factors. Examples of potential financially material ESG Factors include corporate governance, company culture, exposure to climate change, human capital management. To assess ESG Factors, portfolio management uses issuer reports, third-party data, and internally-generated analyses and may engage directly with issuers. ESG Factors are one of many considerations in the investment decision-making process and may not be determinative in deciding to include or exclude an investment from the portfolio.

ESG Integration Risk. There is a risk that considering ESG Factors as part of a Fund’s investment process may fail to produce the intended results or that each Fund may perform differently from funds that have a similar investment style but do not formally incorporate such considerations in their strategy. Information related to ESG Factors provided by issuers and third parties, upon which portfolio management may utilize, continues to develop, and may be incomplete, inaccurate, use different methodologies or be applied differently across issuers and industries. Increased portfolio turnover may result in higher expenses and potentially higher net taxable gains or losses.

Bloomberg 1-3 Year U.S. Government/Credit Index measures Treasuries, government-related issues and corporates with maturity between 1-3 years. Index performance does not reflect the expenses of managing a portfolio as an index is unmanaged and not available for direct investment.

data.title: QYLD Vs. SPYI: I Prefer QYLD For 2 Reasons
data.description:
QYLD and SPYI ETF: previous thesis and new developments

The last time I analyzed the Global X NASDAQ 100 Covered Call ETF (NASDAQ:QYLD) was about a month ago (on 1-15-2025 to be exact). That article was titled “QYLD: Buy The Fear As Volatility Returns”. In that article, I rated the ETF as a buy as I saw the surge of volatility as a positive for the fund. Quote:

I am upgrading Global X NASDAQ 100 Covered Call ETF to 'Buy' due to my anticipation of the return of market volatility. Higher volatility can boost option premiums for QYLD but tends to dampen the return potential of equity. My assessment is geared toward the near term only, and I suggest investors only use the Fund as a tactical tool. Long-term return is ultimately driven by growth, and QYLD's option overlay puts a cap on the return from growth.

As for the Neos S&P 500(R) High Income ETF (BATS:SPYI), I last wrote on its in May 2024. My last article was titled "SPYI ETF: Prepare For The Return Of Volatility”. As you can tell from the title, the article also placed an emphasis on the impact of volatility on the fund’s return/risk profile. More specifically, I rated the fund as HOLD based on the following considerations:

Market volatility is currently quiet, with the S&P VIX Index near its 52 weeks and far below its average over a longer period. I anticipate that market volatility will increase due to factors such as inflation, the Fed's future policies, and geopolitical tensions. The Neos S&P 500(R) High Income ETF could be a sound investment idea as it uses options to generate high monthly income and provides a hedge against a higher VIX.

Two considerations promoted this follow-up article on these funds. First, since the publications of these articles, there have been some changes in the key parameters surrounding these ETFs with the top one being the volatility index again. Both of my previous articles placed an emphasis on the VIX index for a good reason – as the VIX is a key driver of their income generation. Later, in the article, I will explain why the current implied volatility (IV) underlying these funds are a bit more favorable for SPYI. Yet, I see two factors that can offset this factor and thus still feel more bullish toward QYLD: QYLD’s lower fees and also its lower correlation with the broader market.

Second, since both QYLD and SPYI are ETFs that aim to provide income to investors by employing similar strategies, they can appear interchangeably for many investors. Indeed, if you just look at the performance in the past (as you can see from the following chart), their price moves almost entirely overlap. Thus, I feel a side-by-side comparison of these funds could be very helpful for potential investors to better see the similarities and differences between them.

QYLD and SPYI ETF: similarities and differences

Before diving in, let me summarize the basics of these funds for investors new to them. The table below presents the envelope information for QYLD and SPYI. As seen, QYLD, incepted in December 2013, has a much longer history than SPYI, which was launched more recently in 2022. QYLD also has a much larger Asset Under Management (AUM) of $8.98 billion, almost triple SPYI's $3.02 billion. Both are classified as "Nontraditional Equity" funds because they both utilize a covered call strategy as detailed in their fund description quoted below. The key difference is that they track different indices, with QYLD focusing on the Nasdaq 100 and SPYI on the S&P 500.

QYLD fund description: QYLD seeks yield from the Nasdaq-100 via options premium. Historically, investors came to the Nasdaq for growth, not yield. Enter QYLD, which provides Nasdaq-100 exposure but earns income by selling call options and passes it on to investors net of fees. Covered call ETFs are hardly new, but QYLD was the first to apply it to the Nasdaq-100. Expect a generally less volatile return pattern from QYLD relative to ETFs tracking the Nasdaq-100.

SPYI fund description: SPYI aims for tax-efficient and high monthly income by actively investing in stocks and options on the S&P 500 Index. The fund employs a call spread approach that uses SPX index option futures contracts.

They have slightly different expense ratios, with QYLD at 0.61% and SPYI slightly higher at 0.68%. Both ETFs’ primary goal is to provide income, and price appreciation is only a secondary goal - or even a tertiary goal in my view. In my consideration of these funds, their secondary goal is to provide diversification away from the broader market. But I am ahead of myself here and will revisit this point in the next section. With appreciation potential as a less important factor, I don’t see the point of paying higher fee, which is the first reason that I prefer QYLD.

QYLQ and SPYI ETF: correlation with the broader market

As just mentioned, in my consideration of these funds (or other similar funds using option overlay), a crucial goal is to provide diversification away from the broader market – which is very overvalued in my view. An option overlay helps to hedge if/when a valuation correction helps. If you think along this line too, then QYLD does (or did) a much better job than SPYI. As an example, the next table displays the correlations among SPYI, QYLD, and also SPY (which is used to approximate the border market here) since the inception of SPYI. As seen, SPYI shows a strong correlation of 0.98 with the SPY, providing little diversification. In contrast, QYLD has historically demonstrated a substantially lower correlation of 0.85 with SPY.

Other risks and final thoughts

As an additional positive, QYLD’s annualized standard deviation of 10.02% is actually lower than SPYI’s 10.87%, despite QYLD’s higher historical returns and also its underlying index’s higher volatility.

Now, in terms of negatives, a generic downside risk for both QYLD and SPYI is that the use of options limits their price appreciation potential. As seen, in the table above, their annualized returns are both far below the return of SPY, which delivered an annualized return of 20.82%.

A downside risk more specific QYLD, the IV of its underlying index (which is approximated by QQQ here) is slightly lower than that of SPYI (which is approximated by SPY here). This means that QYLD’s options are a bit underpaid compared to SPYI’s in my model. More specifically, the chart below shows that the current IV for QQQ is 16.6, which is in the 39th percentile of its one-year range, meaning it has been lower 39% of the time over the past year. It is also 15.3% lower than its 20-day historical volatility (HV).

SPY’s IV is also below historical averages, but to a lesser extent as you can see from the following chart. To wit, the current IV for SPY is 11.8, placing it in the 37th percentile rank over the past year, meaning it was lower 37% of the time. It is 13.9% lower than the 20-day HV.

However, my overall assessment is that the differences in their IV discounts are a relatively minor factor. The more important issues in my mind are again A) their income generation ability net of fees (BTW, QYLD’s current yield of 12.2% is also noticeably higher than SPYI’s 11.8%), and B) their correlation with the overall market. On both considerations, I see QYLD as a better choice than SPYI. As such, I rate QYLD as BUY and SPYI as a HOLD under current conditions.

data.title: XPO Is Well-Positioned For Market Volatility But Overpriced
data.description:
The logistics industry sees a mixed market conditions. Truckloads or TL continue to thrive with a solid demand that highlights capacity constraints. Meanwhile, less-than-truckloads or LTL still face challenges as domestic consumption remains soft. Despite all this, some sprinkles of hope are evident.

In this article, I would like to cover another company in the freight and logistics sector, XPO, Inc. (NYSE: XPO). Its decent topline performance and prudent business portfolio mix are some of my key considerations for taking an interest in this company. It also has good liquidity, making it well-positioned for any disruptions and volatility.

And as for its price, the stock is quite expensive. Upside potential is limited, so entering can be challenging. I also advise readers to trade with caution as technicals suggest some downside risk.

Q424: XPO’s Topline Resilience

XPO, Inc. was a bit hammered during Q424 as the LTL market remained soft. These were evident in XPO’s report.

XPO’s sales amounted to $1.92B, down by -1% or $19M from $1.94B in Q424. Like its peers, the decrease was primarily due to the lower demand for LTLs in its North American segment. We can see it in its lower volume during the quarter. For instance, the average pounds per day its LTLs carried fell by -6% from 69,357 to 65,433. In addition, the average shipments per day were only 49,109, which was about 4% lower than in the same quarter in FY23. This shows the weaker consumption in the US as inflation stayed stubborn. While the trend has become more manageable, we must note that the general price level has already increased substantially since FY22. Also note its uptick from 2.6% in October to 2.9% in December, which affected domestic consumption further.

Despite this, it was able to implement strategic pricing to lower the unfavorable impact of LTL revenues. As we can see, its average revenue per shipment without fuel surcharges rose to $325 versus $307. This translated into a 2% increase in shipment revenue from $966M to $985M. However, it was easily offset by the -22% decrease in its lower fuel surcharges. Over its North American segment revenue decreased by 3%.

On the flip side, its European segment showed more stability. Its segment revenue reached $765M, up by 2% from $753 in Q424. In a report, it was estimated that the freight industry in the region rose by nearly 1% to €428B in FY24 after a -3% decrease in FY23 despite the geopolitical tension between Russia and Ukraine.

What I like most about XPO is its operational efficiency. Its margins remained stable with a slight increase- Gross Profit Margin at 16.2% from 16.1% in Q423 and Operating Margin at 9.0% from 8.0%. In addition, its operating leverage decreased from 92% to 91%. This is a crucial aspect in making XPO well-positioned for the current market environment. Its lower cost sensitivity and variable cost contribution will allow it to be more flexible to market changes. It also means the impact of its direct costs like fuel also slightly decreased. This can be due to lower Brent and WTI crude oil prices. In addition, the company was also able to buy and/or save more fuel as oil output increased.

Concerning some of its peers, you can refer to my recent coverage of ODFL. You can see that XPO had one of the most decent performances during the quarter. Its revenue decrease was still more manageable than the peer average. Also, its operating margin ranked third along with TFII, another stock I covered.

Things I Like About XPO

Despite the notable risks in the market, I think XPO is still a good addition to our portfolio for these reasons.

High Liquidity and Continued Investment

XPO has a solid financial positioning, which allows it to remain sustainable despite erratic market conditions. Its cash and cash equivalents are stable and adequate to cover all its current portion of borrowings and capital lease obligations. While it appears small relative to the total assets, we must remember that XPO is an asset-heavy company. Over 50% of its assets are fixed assets. Also, it has high receivables, showing it continues to earn from its operations. The combined value of cash and receivables is $1.2B, which can cover nearly 90% of all current liabilities.

Meanwhile, borrowing levels are high, showing XPO’s heavier reliance on debt financing than equity. Nonetheless, the general level is stable since 99% of the total amount will not mature within the year. Over 50% of the total borrowings will not mature until FY28, so XPO has enough time to repay it. But what’s more important is that XPO is still earning enough to sustain repayments. With a 2.52 Net Debt/EBITDA, XPO can make a single payment for all its borrowings within two years.

Lastly, we can see in its cash flow statement that all cash from operating activities can cover its capital expenditures. It still generates a positive number, which can be added to cash reserves or used to pay borrowings with current maturities. Note that it invested in $715M in fixed assets, about 15% higher than FY23, it was also about five times larger than pre-pandemic values.

Business Mix

Another thing I like about XPO is its international market presence and the fact that it does not completely rely on LTLs. In its European segment, it also offers truckload services and brokerage. So, a weakness in one segment or location can be balanced by a stronger segment or area. And although LTL remains its crown jewel, its other business segments can partially offset its soft performance. That was why its revenue decrease was less than 1%.

Young Truck Age

After allotting over $700M to capital expenditures, XPO got 2,400 new tractors. On top of that, the average of its tractors went down to 4.1 years old. This is over 1 year younger than the five-year average of 5.4 years. This is also much lower than the typical useful age of LTLs at 10–12 years and TLs at 8-10 years. So, having new tractors means lower chances of maintenance, more efficient driving and fuel use, and a safer journey for XPO’s drivers.

Other Aspects

These are some areas of consideration for XPO and its peers.

Lower Brent and WTI Prices

We have seen Brent and WTI move in a downward trend in the past year. While there were some upticks driven by OPEC cuts, the general pattern is decreasing. This year, we can expect their prices to decrease further as OPEC plans to raise oil output.

While these can impact fuel surcharges negatively, there are some positive things we can derive from them. Lower oil prices can also lower fuel costs for trucking companies. In addition, it can lead to a multiplier effect in the long run. It can help lower the general price level to bolster demand. As a result, there may be more demand for LTLs and TLs to transport goods.

The Rise of E-Commerce

With the rise of e-commerce, many buyers do not have to frequently visit brick-and-mortar stores. Almost everything can be found and done online. Aside from online shops, digital banking and wallets allow virtual transactions.

Recent data show rosy results, where over 80% of people shopped online in the past month. This year, about 2.8B people are expected to shop online. Note that the study also revealed 64% of online shoppers spent at least $200 a month. Hence, this uptrend can help drive the LTL market recovery.

I would also like to note that Amazon (AMZN) and Walmart (WMT) have their own logistics services. But most online shops still don’t have that network capacity to have their own. Instead, they are getting services from third-party providers to save time and investment costs on LTLs. This can boost the demand for XPO and its peers in the US. The country accounted for $1.2B or 20% of the global e-commerce revenue.

Solid TL Market, Recovering LTL Market

Truckload saw its hype last year as the rise in exports and imports boosted their demand. This was more evident during the second half as its producer price index or PPI rebounded to the 174-178 range. Last December, the PPI reached its thirteen-month high at 178.04. The challenge now is capacity constraints, which may widen the demand gap.

Meanwhile, the LTL market remains soft but resilient. The slight PPI rebound to 410.41 during Q424 showed some opportunities that helped stabilize the market. One is the capacity constraints in the TL market. LTLs supported goods mobility to maintain the supply chain. In addition, the expanding e-commerce, particularly smaller businesses, boosted LTL demand.

This year, mixing TLs and LTLs can be prudent. That’s why I think XPO is a few steps ahead of the market. Its business portfolio mix and younger tractor age can help it capture more TL demand and prepare for the LTL market recovery.

Price Ratios

Using company EPS and book value per share, we can see that the stock is undervalued. The uptrend is reasonable, given its stable operations and the potential LTL market recovery. It also keeps expanding with its increased and younger tractors. However, the actual rate of increase is still unwarranted if we use historical averages. At its price, XPO is trading about 45x its earnings and 11x its BVPS. What’s more noticeable is that the stock is trading about 50% higher than the five-year average. This makes XPO expensive today relative to its value in previous years. The TP of $92.01 and $77.85 shows the stock may still decrease by at least 30%. Nonetheless, you must note that these price ratios do not assess the intrinsic value of XPO. What we are doing is more on historical comparison of its cheapness or expensiveness.

DCF Model

To have a more accurate valuation of XPO, I also used the DCF Model. Like the two price ratios, it also showed that XPO is overpriced. We got a TP of $110.84, which suggests a 24% downside risk.

I derived its perpetual growth rate just by setting it at 2.00%. As you can see, it is higher than ODFL. One of the things I considered is its presence in the European TL market. While the former concentrates solely on LTLs, the latter offers both tractors. The TL market remains solid, which can boost XPO’s performance this FY25. In addition, I also tried to make it consistent with the Fed’s inflation target.

For the WACC, I derived it using the Capital Asset Pricing Model. I know 5.40% may sound low since equities are more volatile and that rate is closer to bond yields. Yet, we must understand that its financial leverage is primarily borrowings. Most of its borrowings are senior notes with fixed yields, so I think the derived value is still reasonable.

Lastly, I derived FCFF by projecting income statement values from revenue down to operating expenses. By adding back depreciation, I got EBITDA and deducted corporate income tax from it to derive NOPAT. Then, I added and subtracted my projected depreciation, capex, and net working capital.

Technicals

XPO’s momentum weakened after its recent pullback. It is important to note some indications of a potential reversal. First is the appearance of three black crows after peaking at $159 last December 17. After landing at $131 on December 31. It consolidated for a few weeks and rebounded to $140 before dropping to $128 last February. It rebounded again and jumped to $149. Now, the price is consolidating again. However, we can see that its bullish momentum is no longer as strong as before its peak. It already created two lower lows. Its most recent high failed to hit the resistance.

For the MACD, the momentum is still there. But again, it is already weaker than the previous peak. The MACD line remains above the signal line. The histogram stays green and positive. Yet, yesterday’s indicators showed a weaker performance. This suggests that trading is still possible, but risks must be considered.

The RSI also suggests that trading gains are still possible. It is still below the oversold region. In addition, historical RSIs peaked at 80 before dropping again.

To give you a trading tip, we can apply the Fibonacci retracement. The current price is very near the 0.786 retracement point. From there, we can note more changes. The most recent dip is also near the 0.50 retracement. If we use this to make a move, traders can wait for the price to dip to $124, which is the 0.386 retracement. Coincidentally, this is the stop loss point of our 0.5:1.5 reward-risk ratio, proving the possibility of a dip to this value.

Conclusion

XPO, Inc. keeps solid topline performance with its cost-efficiency, pricing strategy, and prudent business mix. It is also well-positioned for potential market recovery and/or volatility with its high liquidity and new tractors. The concern right now is the stock appears expensive as shown by our valuation techniques. In addition, technical indicators suggest potential dips, so trading at its current level can be risky. So, I am recommending this stock as a hold.

data.title: Janus Henderson Responsible International Dividend Fund Q4 2024 Commentary
data.description:
Investment environment

During the quarter, global equity markets were flat in U.S. dollar (USDOLLAR,DXY) terms, although international markets declined. Donald Trump’s election win spurred the U.S. market to record highs on hopes the new administration would be more proactive about promoting U.S. economic growth. Elsewhere, concerns that Trump’s policies might negatively impact other countries’ growth due to trade tariffs and changes in foreign policy proved to be a headwind. Political turmoil in Germany and France also weighed on sentiment, with European equities lagging their U.S. counterparts. The “Magnificent 7” stocks – the seven dominant tech companies Apple (AAPL), Microsoft (MSFT), Amazon (AMZN), Alphabet (GOOG,GOOGL), META, Nvidia (NVDA), and Tesla (TSLA) – contributed to relative performance, driven by optimism about artificial intelligence. Growth stocks notably outperformed value stocks. Higher yields weighed on defensive sectors such as real estate, consumer staples, and utilities, but were positive for the banking sector, which contributed to relative results. Asian equities weakened over the quarter as anxiety about U.S.-China trade tensions hurt sentiment. Chinese shares retreated amid uncertainty about President-elect Trump’s policies and concerns that stimulus measures would fail to deliver the expected economic boost.

Investors started anticipating fewer interest rate cuts and potentially higher inflation in coming years, with government bond yields rising over the quarter despite rate cuts by the U.S. Federal Reserve (Fed), European Central Bank, and the Bank of England. The U.S. dollar strengthened notably over the quarter versus other currencies after the Fed hinted at fewer rate cuts for 2025 than the market expected.

Portfolio review

Within the information technology sector, investor optimism around the adoption of AI drove up the share price of chip manufacturer Taiwan Semiconductor (TSM), which has benefited from the capital investment deployed by its customers to develop the company’s AI capabilities. This positive contribution to relative performance was partially offset within IT by the portfolio’s holding in Samsung Electronics (OTCPK:SSNLF), whose share price fell after the company delayed the release of a new semiconductor chip, leading to investor fears that it had missed the AI product cycle.

However, we remain confident the company can recover and believe the stock is attractively valued at current levels.

In the consumer discretionary sector, SONY’s share price benefited from earnings upgrades, driven by the outlook for

its music, games, and films divisions – where in the latter, a number of strong film releases are expected in 2025.

The quarter proved challenging for European financial stocks, with the portfolio’s holding in Dutch bank ING weighing on relative results. Additionally, concerns about Chinese economic growth and the weak domestic consumer environment impacted the holding in Hong Kong-based insurer AIA, which underperformed. This was partially offset by strong performance from our UK bank NatWest, which produced solid returns over 2024, thanks in part to significant restructuring. We believe the company should see further progress in 2025.

Manager outlook

Now that inflation appears to be under control, centrals banks have proceeded to cut interest rates in many countries

around the world, and some governments are looking at ways to stimulate growth. These factors should be positive for companies and consumers as we head into 2025.

However, we remain cognizant of the geopolitical backdrop and recognize that the new U.S. government brings some uncertainty, which could result in market volatility and highlights the importance of maintaining a diversified portfolio.

We remain confident that the companies held in the portfolio can navigate the current economic environment, aided by strong balance sheets and cash-generating capabilities.

Shareholder returns, announced in terms of both dividends and share buybacks, remain positive, which suggests that management teams share our confidence in the long-term outlooks for their companies.

Portfolio management

Ben Lofthouse, CFA | Faizan Baig, CFA

data.title: Janus Henderson Research Fund Q4 2024 Commentary
data.description:
Investment environment

Equity markets posted further gains in the fourth quarter, following strong performance through the first three quarters of 2024. The U.S. presidential election triggered a strong rally in U.S. stocks. Stocks encountered volatility in December, however, after the Federal Reserve (Fed) cut its policy rate but indicated fewer- than-expected rate reductions for 2025. Economic activity in the U.S. and globally remained relatively solid, while earnings continued to grow for many companies. Meanwhile, major economies outside the U.S. struggled to find their footing, as reflected by mixed economic data across Europe and Asia.

Portfolio review

Relative performance was hindered by our position in ASML Holding (ASML), a leading global supplier of lithography equipment used in semiconductor production. The stock declined in October on near-term revenue growth concerns after the company disclosed weaker-than-expected bookings. It also faced uncertainty over planned capital spending cuts by chipmaker Intel and potential U.S. government restrictions on exports of lithography equipment to China, a major ASML market. The stock regained some ground later in the fourth quarter following a constructive Investor Day, when ASML management gave a positive outlook on the lithography market while providing robust 2030 growth and margin targets. We have continued to see durable competitive advantages for ASML, the sole global supplier of extreme ultraviolet lithography machines used to manufacture the most advanced semiconductor chips.

Building products supplier Ferguson (FERG) was another relative detractor. Ferguson reported weaker-than-expected quarterly results due to weak sales, reduced margins, and increased capital investment. The company also faced challenges as the prospect of higher-than-expected interest rates in 2025 created uncertainty for the construction sector. We believe the market response to its recent results was overstated, and we continue to see long-term opportunity given the company's strong competitive positioning and solid balance sheet.

We benefited from our position in Broadcom (AVGO), a global technology company that designs, develops, and supplies semiconductor and infrastructure software solutions. The company has established a strong track record of robust margins, strong free-cash-flow generation, and best-in-class capital returns. Broadcom has been a beneficiary of increased spending on semiconductors to support the rollout of artificial intelligence. It has also experienced stable revenue growth from other areas of the semiconductor market not driven by AI-related investments. Broadcom reported better-than-expected quarterly earnings and offered a 2027 AI market outlook that significantly exceeded expectations.

Liberty Media, another contributor, owns motor racing company Formula One Group (FWONA). The company has been working to build a higher profile for Formula One racing in the U.S., leading to rapid audience growth and increased consumer engagement. Going forward, we believe the company has multiple avenues for increasing revenue growth and shareholder value as it seeks to renegotiate race promotion fees, sponsorship deals, and global streaming rights on more advantageous terms. We see added opportunity around its planned acquisition of Grand Prix motorcycle racing company MotoGP.

Manager outlook

The combination of recent interest rate cuts and other potential accommodative policies in the U.S. and stimulus in China should lend support to the global economy. Large-cap equity markets have been quick to price in the cycle’s extension, leaving valuations vulnerable to an uneven labor market, the threat of higher long-term rates, and the policy uncertainty that comes with a new U.S. administration.

Meanwhile, major economies outside the U.S. have struggled to find their footing, with mixed data across Europe and Asia. Despite these crosscurrents, investors are entering 2025 in a relatively constructive mood. For our part, we are monitoring pockets of weakness in some subsectors and countries, and we are mindful of the lagged risks of higher interest rates in some areas of consumer credit. Geopolitical developments continue to take a human toll and could potentially have a significant economic impact while contributing to investment market volatility.

We remain committed to our fundamentals-driven investment strategy. This strategy is driven by our strong and experienced analyst team members, who bring deep knowledge of their respective sectors. Their proprietary fundamental research helps identify opportunities that we believe are underappreciated by the market. Our analyst team is constantly on the lookout for companies with strong or improving business models, good management teams, and durable competitive advantages. We continue to find companies with compelling growth opportunities that arise from innovation, market share gains, and strong growth in their end markets. Through this disciplined investment approach, we will continue to pursue our goal of long-term growth in capital.

Portfolio management

Joshua Cummings, CFA | John Jordan

data.title: Eggs, And A Whole Lot More
data.description:
Every election cycle has its own peculiar trope at the center of the narrative, the very tangible thing that brings swirling abstract narratives into recognizably defined three-dimensional space.

For the 2024 campaign, that thing was the price of eggs. The complexities of global supply chains, the arcane accounting practices for billions of dollars of government expenditure, the extreme distortions of consumer demand trends during and after the Covid pandemic – all the many variables involved in the highest levels of inflation experienced since the 1970s boiled down to one simple formulation: Have you seen the price of eggs lately? Yes they had, and they were not happy about it, and they made their point on November 5.

The Chickens and the Eggs

Given the prominent place afforded last year to that one food category as a proxy for the entire inflation story, it is perhaps an unfortunate coincidence that eggs are back in the news recently for reasons unrelated to the inflationary forces at play for much of the past three years.

The spread of avian flu has caused havoc among the chicken farms from which our Grade-A cartons begin their long journey to our kitchen tables. The price of eggs today is roughly double what it was a year ago, and soared by 15 percent in the month of January alone.

The flu has necessitated the slaughter of millions of chickens, drastically reducing supply, and the magnitude of the resulting price increases for eggs accounted for fully two-thirds of the total increase in food prices last month, according to the latest Consumer Price Index report issued by the Bureau of Labor Statistics this past week.

But it’s not just about eggs. The Fed doesn’t pay as much attention to the volatile categories of food and energy products as it does to so-called core inflation, the more stable baskets of goods and services like apparel, medical care and shelter. The problem is, these categories are proving to be stubborn as well.

Gimme Shelter

Non-food and energy services, in particular, have not come down at the rate the Fed was expecting to see a year ago. For the last twelve months, prices for shelter (rent and owner’s equivalent) have increased 4.4 percent while transportation services, of which airline travel is a major component, have gone up 8.0 percent.

That has kept the core CPI number (the gray dotted line in the above chart) stuck at or around 3.3 percent since the middle of last summer. The Fed went ahead with its first rate cut in September regardless, thinking that the numbers would continue to decline, but here we are in February and 3.3 percent is still the number for core inflation.

And That’s Without New Tariffs

The seeming intractability of inflation alone would strongly suggest a continued pause by the Fed, with no rate cuts likely for the foreseeable future. But that, of course, doesn’t take into account whatever tariffs are eventually going to make their way into law.

As we noted in our commentary last week, the only consistent aspect of the new administration’s tariff policy so far is its inconsistency, with policymakers saying one thing one day, watching how markets react, and saying something else the next day (or the next hour).

The word in vogue this week has been “reciprocal tariffs,” which sounds like a sort of tit-for-tat policy aimed at matching the tariffs other countries set on products we currently import.

Or is it? Does it include things like the 20 percent VAT applied to many European exports? How about structural barriers on products from places like Japan and India? And when? The latest indications point to April (maybe) as the likely start date (unless something else comes up in the meantime, and notwithstanding carve-outs and exemptions and all the rest of the baroque ornaments of this administration’s approach to tariffs).

All this remains to be seen, and markets have yet to exhibit any overriding concerns. Meanwhile, interest rates are likely to remain where they are, and vegan baking recipes that call for egg substitutes are likely to grow in popularity.

data.title: Janus Henderson Overseas ADR Managed Account Q4 2024 Commentary
data.description:
Investment environment

Non-U.S. equities delivered negative returns in the fourth quarter, as a strong dollar, increased geopolitical uncertainty, and a weaker outlook for the Eurozone economy pressured investor sentiment toward international shares. The U.S. presidential election triggered a broad rotation out of international stocks and into the U.S. market, as investors anticipated a strong pro-American agenda and potential protectionist trade policies under Donald Trump’s presidency. The sell-off in non-U.S. stocks was often indiscriminate and left many stocks trading at discounted multiples that we believe do not accurately reflect business prospects or the potential for free-cash-flow growth.

Portfolio review

Stock selection lifted the portfolio’s relative performance in a challenging fourth quarter. Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company (TSM) was a top relative contributor. As the world’s largest contract chip manufacturer, TSMC is a major fabricator of logic semiconductors including graphic processing units critical to the deployment of generative artificial intelligence. As its competitors have faltered, TSMC has built on its market leadership, leading to increased pricing power and margins. Partnerships with large technology companies and hyperscalers have also supported its revenue growth, helping TSMC report even stronger-than-expected earnings and increased guidance.

Liberty Media, another contributor, owns a controlling interest in motor racing company Formula One Group (FWONA). Liberty Media’s efforts to build a higher profile for Formula One racing, especially in the U.S., have resulted in increased viewership and advertising revenues. Going forward, we believe the company may have multiple avenues to grow revenues and shareholder value as it looks to renegotiate race promotion fees, sponsorship deals, and global streaming rights on more advantageous terms. We see added opportunity around the company’s acquisition of Grand Prix motorcycle racing company MotoGP.

Teck Resources (TECK) was a prominent detractor. This Canadian mining company produces copper, zinc, and other materials. Teck struggled against a backdrop of weaker copper prices, slower economic growth in China, and a strong U.S. dollar. Longer term, we see strong demand drivers for copper, a key material required for green energy applications and to meet AI-powered demand for carbon neutral electricity sources. We believe the mining industry will struggle to produce enough copper to meet this demand, leading to higher copper prices. In our view, Teck is well positioned to benefit from these trends due to its attractive production pipeline and relatively low capital requirements.

ICON (ICLR), another detractor, is a multinational provider of outsourced drug and medical device development and clinical testing services. ICON disappointed investors by reporting weaker-than-expected revenue growth, partly because of the cancellation of some new drug development orders. Customers, especially in the biotechnology space, have also been more cautious about their spending on clinical trials, which led ICON to lower guidance. Despite near-term uncertainty, we continue to believe ICON is well positioned to benefit as more drug and medical device companies outsource their drug trials.

Manager outlook

We anticipate U.S. policy under the incoming Trump administration could have significant implications for global investment markets. Some Trump policies, such as corporate tax cuts, may be positive drivers of economic and corporate earnings growth. We see greater uncertainty around other policy proposals, such as higher tariffs aimed at China but potentially Mexico, Canada, and the European Union. Such tariffs may invite retaliation and could worsen inflation and lead to higher interest rates. We anticipate we could see some volatility as investors wait for clarity on what policies the Trump administration will be able to enact, and how such policies might impact the broader global economy.

Rather than try to predict broader fiscal and monetary policy shifts, we will look to use volatility to our advantage as we seek out opportunities to put our bottom-up stock selection to work. We remain focused on identifying pockets of opportunity around the world where we believe fundamentals and free-cash-flow growth are not fully reflected in stock valuations. We have identified such opportunities in a diverse range of countries and industries including European and Japanese financials that may benefit from higher-for- longer interest rates. We have also found attractive investments in consumer companies with strong global brands, as well as with manufacturers of key components that support AI. Many of these component makers have been beneficiaries of AI-related spending but trade at much more attractive valuations relative to large, high-profile U.S. technology companies. As we see these opportunities, we continue to pay close attention to the quality of balance sheets, management teams, and corporate strategy. We view this disciplined, fundamentals-driven approach as key to delivering long-term capital appreciation for our investors.

Portfolio management

Julian McManus | Christopher O'Malley, CFA

data.title: FVAL: A High-Tech Value Play With Risky Downside Exposure
data.description:
ETF Overview

Fidelity Value Factor ETF (NYSEARCA:FVAL) invests in a portfolio of about 130 U.S. large-cap and mid-cap value stocks. The fund’s expense ratio of 0.15% is comparable to other factor-based funds. For example, Fidelity Quality Factor ETF (FQAL) has a slightly higher expense ratio of 0.16%. FVAL’s sector neutral approach has resulted in its higher exposure to the technology sector relative to other value funds. This improves its growth profile, but its long-term growth return still trails the S&P 500 index. On the other hand, FVAL is underweight in defensive sectors relative to other value funds, making it more vulnerable in market downturns. Therefore, FVAL appears to be an awkward ETF, as investors seeking higher returns might opt for the S&P 500, while those wanting better downside protection may consider other value funds.

Fund Analysis

FVAL’s construction methodology

Let us look at how FVAL constructs its portfolio. FVAL basically tracks the Fidelity U.S. Value Factor Index. This index selects stocks based on a factor-based value approach. As can be seen from the table below, four value factors that are used to determine which stocks are to be selected include: free cash flow yield (strong cash generation), EBITDA to EV ratio (how efficient the company operates relative to valuation), tangible book value to price (asset-backed valuation), and forward earnings yield (future earnings expectations).

Overweight in technology but underweight in defensive sectors

FVAL’s portfolio construction has resulted in a portfolio of value stocks. But its sector composition is very different than other value funds. Below is a table that shows FVAL’s sector allocation. As can be seen from the table, the technology sector represents about 30.6% of its total portfolio. This is unusually high relative to other traditional value funds. For example, Vanguard Value ETF (VTV) and iShares S&P Core Value ETF (IUSV) have lower technology stock exposures of 11.8% and 23.4% respectively. This is because FVAL tries to maintain sector neutrality relative to the broader market (e.g. S&P 500 index), and technology stocks represent nearly one third of the S&P 500 index. This explains why FVAL has very similar technology sector exposure to the S&P 500 index.

On the other hand, FVAL’s exposure to defensive sectors is quite limited. This is also due to its sector neutrality approach. As the table above shows, consumer defensive, healthcare, utilities sectors represent about 17.9% of its total portfolio. This is similar to the S&P 500 index’s exposure (about 19.1%). In contrast, VTV and IUSV have much higher exposure to defensive sectors at 31.3% and 26.1% respectively.

FVAL has outperformed VTV and IUSV in the past

FVAL has delivered a total return of 191.4% since its inception back in September 2016. In contrast, VTV and IUSV delivered inferior total returns of 150.8% and 148.7% respectively. FVAL’s outperformance in the past was likely due to its higher exposure towards the technology sector. As we know, the technology sector has been one of the fastest growing sectors among all other sectors in the S&P 500 index thanks to technology advancement. The sector is poised to deliver strong growth in the upcoming decades as well, fueled by many technological megatrends such as artificial intelligence, high-performance computing, AR/VRs, robotics, etc. Therefore, it is very likely that FVAL will continue to outperform VTV and IUSV in the foreseeable future.

Now that some might ask whether FVAL will match the performance of the S&P 500 index given its similar exposure to technology stock. The answer is likely not. This is because FVAL’s portfolio is tilted towards value and has fewer growth stocks than the S&P 500 index. As can be seen from the chart below, value stocks represent about 44% of FVAL’s portfolio, while growth stocks only represent about 8%. In contrast, growth stocks represent about 21% of the S&P 500 index, while value stocks only represent about 27% of the index. Therefore, FVAL still has a weaker growth profile than the S&P 500 index.

We can also observe this in the past, where the S&P 500 index’s total return of 224.5% outperformed FVAL’s 191.4% since FVAL’s inception.

FVAL has higher downside risk

FVAL has been through 3 major corrections since its inception: late 2018, 2020, and 2022. As can be seen from the chart below, the fund has typically fallen more than its peer funds VTV and IUSV. Therefore, FVAL’s construction methodology does not result in better downside protection. In terms of volatility, FVAL has a beta ratio of 0.98 relative to the S&P 500 index. For reader’s information, a beta ratio above 1 means the fund is more volatile than the S&P 500 index. In contrast, VTV and IUSV have beta ratios of 0.83 and 0.92 respectively.

FVAL’s higher downside risk is not difficult to explain. As we know, many technology stocks are heavily influenced by market sentiments, and as expectations change, they tend to experience higher volatilities, and this may lead to higher downside risk. Therefore, FVAL's exposure to technology stocks will inevitably result in its greater volatility and higher downside risk.

Investor Takeaway

FVAL’s significant exposure to technology stocks has driven its outperformance and improved its growth outlook relative to other value funds. However, FVAL is also vulnerable in market downturns. This makes FVAL a somewhat awkward ETF to own. For investors seeking higher returns, why not simply invest in the S&P 500 funds? For investors wanting better downside protection in market downturns, many other value funds appear to be better choices. Therefore, we see no real advantage of owning FVAL over the S&P 500 index and other value funds.
